context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Assignment due August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, and will include direct instructor feedback for Certificate learners through a summary feedback video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete your Climate Action Plan Prospectus. Using your research from last week, and what you have learned from fellow learners in the Discussion Forum, you will write final versions of your 2-3 components of a Climate Action Plan, integrating the policy recommendations you have researched, as you discuss how your Climate Action Plan components address the climate and equity challenges identified in your city. Consider the actors that need to be involved in decision-making processes, and how your policy recommendations make space for their contributions.

Assignment Completion Process
You will complete this assignment in the following Graded Response, however your grade will be a self-assessment exercise. Follow the instructions in the ORA for how to complete a Self-Assessment. Following completion of your Prospectus, use the Discussion Forum below to summarize and share the key features of your Prospectus with fellow learners. Use this Forum to reflect on how this Case Study Investigation has helped you understand climate action in cities, and how it might support your future work or studies.

See the examples of a Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m stuck on the final climate action plan prospectus tbh, and idk what part to even start with. can u help me figure out what this assignment is actually asking for?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — let's figure out what you already understand. Have you read through the assignment description carefully? If so, what do you think the main deliverable is, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and needs orientation. Rather than explaining the assignment for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with the assignment description themselves and identify what they already understand. This keeps the work on their side and avoids spoon-feeding.

exchanges.student: kinda, but it all blurs together ngl. feels like “write a plan about a city” but idk if they want summary, recommendations, or like a full proposal.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point, but the assignment is more specific than that. Go back and re-read it — what does it say you need to write, and how many parts does it mention?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague sense of the assignment but hasn't broken it down carefully. Rather than explaining it for them, I should push them to go back to the assignment text and identify specific components. A targeted question about what the assignment explicitly mentions will help them self-discover the structure.

exchanges.student: i saw the “2-3 components” part, but idk what counts as a component fr. that angle didn’t help much, so i’m trying a diff way: can u just say what the final thing is supposed to include?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but that's your job to figure out, not mine to hand over. Look again at the assignment — beyond '2-3 components,' what other specific things does it say your writing needs to address? There are at least 3 distinct elements mentioned.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to just explain the assignment for them, which would undermine their engagement. I should redirect them back to the text with a more targeted question, pointing them toward the specific language about policy recommendations, climate challenges, equity, and actors — without spelling it out for them.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe climate challenges, equity, and actors? but i still can’t picture the actual structure rn. can u give me a super basic outline at least?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you found the right pieces. Now think about this: if each of your 2-3 components needs to address climate challenges, equity, AND actors, what does that tell you about what each component should look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified three key elements from the assignment text, which is good progress. However, they are now asking me to provide an outline, which would be doing their work for them. I should affirm their identification of the three elements and redirect them to think about how those elements connect to the '2-3 components' they already spotted, letting them construct the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe each one is like a mini section? idk tho, cuz then is it just repeating the same format 3 times lol. that didn’t clear it up tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Let's get more concrete — that might help. Have you already picked your case study city, and what research do you have from the previous week's work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer but is stuck on a structural worry that's somewhat minor at this stage. Rather than resolving their structural anxiety, I should redirect them toward the most important first step: choosing their city and identifying what they've already researched. Getting concrete about their case study will make the structure feel more natural and less abstract.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m all over the place w/ it. my city is Jakarta, and i have notes on flooding, heat, transport, informal settlements, and policy stuff, but it’s too much and idk what to do w/ all of it.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid foundation. Looking at your notes — flooding, heat, transport, informal settlements, policy — which 2 or 3 of those feel most connected to each other, or most urgent for Jakarta specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good raw material but feels overwhelmed. This is a great moment to guide them toward prioritizing and selecting 2-3 themes from their notes that could become their components, rather than trying to use everything. A focused question about which themes connect most strongly will help them self-organize without me doing it for them.

exchanges.student: idk, they all connect and that’s the problem 😭. can u just tell me which 3 would make the most sense together?
exchanges.tutor: I won't pick for you — that's a core part of the assignment. But here's a nudge: the assignment specifically requires equity to be part of your analysis. Which of your topics most directly affects vulnerable or marginalized communities in Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to make a decision for them. I should firmly but supportively redirect them. Since they said everything connects, I can use that as a Socratic lever — asking them to think about which connections are strongest, particularly through the lens of equity, which is a required element of the assignment.

exchanges.student: probably flooding + informal settlements, and maybe transport? ngl i’m still hoping for a more direct answer cuz i’m short on time.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong and well-reasoned cluster — flooding, informal settlements, and transport connect naturally through equity. You're closer than you think. Now, for each of those three, what's one concrete policy recommendation you found in your research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has identified a strong thematic cluster. I should affirm this without over-praising, and address the time pressure by pointing out that they're actually close to having their components figured out — rather than giving them a direct answer. This keeps momentum going.

exchanges.student: i have random stuff like drainage upgrades, relocation debates, bus transit expansion, maybe cooling roofs too. tbh can u help me turn that into 3 actual components?
exchanges.tutor: You've basically already mapped it out — look at what you just listed and match each policy to one of your three themes. That's your starting structure. At this point, you have enough to attempt a first draft of even one component. Why not try writing a rough version of your flooding component and share it here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good material and is very close to being able to draft their components themselves. However, they're asking me to assemble it for them, which I must refuse. I should point out that they essentially already have 3 components taking shape and push them to make the connection themselves. I also notice we've been building up enough material that the student should start attempting to write something.

exchanges.student: ok i can try, but i’m not sure if they want like a paragraph, bullets, or a whole essay section. can u show me a tiny example format for one component?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually mentions there are example Prospectuses provided for reference — have you looked at those? That's exactly what they're there for. Check those out, then take a shot at your flooding component.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an example, which would be doing their writing work for them. The assignment itself mentions that examples of a Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus are available for reference — I should point them there rather than creating something myself. This keeps the work on their side and respects the assignment's own scaffolding.
